explanation: Recurring communal hoax with no credible evidence. Variants have been debunked by multiple Indian fact-checkers.
No scientific evidence supports 5G causing COVID-19. Global health agencies have debunked this conspiracy.
No robust peer-reviewed evidence proves cow urine cures cancer.

claim: WhatsApp messages can hack your phone just by opening them
explanation: General message-opening claim is false; rare vulnerabilities existed historically but were patched.

claim: Indian government is implanting microchips through vaccines
explanation: No evidence supports microchips in vaccines. This is a global conspiracy narrative.
Garlic is nutritious but has no proven protective effect against COVID-19.

claim: Love Jihad is an organized nationwide conspiracy
explanation: Large-scale coordinated conspiracy evidence is not established in official investigations.

claim: A child-lifting gang is active in this area, do not trust outsiders
explanation: Frequent panic-forward hoax pattern; many such alerts are unverified and have triggered violence.

claim: Doctors are harvesting organs from patients in local hospitals
explanation: Viral versions are generally unverified rumors; claims should be checked with official police and hospital statements.

claim: Drinking hot water every 15 minutes kills coronavirus
explanation: No evidence that periodic hot-water drinking can kill coronavirus in the body.

claim: Government is giving free money if you click this link and share OTP
explanation: Common scam pattern using phishing links and OTP theft.

claim: UNVERIFIED election result screenshots prove vote tampering
explanation: Screenshots without provenance are weak evidence; official election authority releases are required.
